What MLAbDev can do

MLAbDev is a machine-learning platform for predicting and improving antibody developability. It covers the full cycle — from risk scoring new sequences through model building, dataset analysis and in silico engineering — all from a single CLI.

5
PLMs supported
4
Architectures
Any
Assay label
2
Task types
Pre-trained
IPI PSR + DS1
🎯
Score new antibodies
Instant PSR / SEC / HIC risk scores using IPI or DS1 pre-trained models. No training needed. Score a single PLM or get a multi-PLM consensus in one command.
Pre-trained PSR · SEC · HIC Multi-PLM
🏗️
Build your own model
Train models on any Excel dataset with any binary or continuous label. CDR3-cluster split prevents leakage. K-fold CV gives unbiased AUC estimates.
Any assay Classification Regression

Understand your dataset
Motif discovery with k-mer / biophysical models. Spearman ρ correlation analysis. t-SNE visualisation. Boxplots of CDR3 charge, length and hydrophobicity.
SHAP t-SNE Biophysics
⚡
Engineer better antibodies
In silico CDR3 mutagenesis: scan every point substitution and rank by predicted ΔP(PASS). Multi-PLM consensus narrows to the highest-confidence mutations.
Mutagenesis IG heatmap Prescreening

Input file format

Excel (.xlsx) — required columns and label conventions
File format Input files must be Excel (.xlsx). CSV is not supported. Column names are case-sensitive. Extra columns are ignored and passed through to the output.

Required and auto-detected columns

ColumnRequiredDescription
BARCODEYes Unique antibody identifier. Used as row key in all output files.
HSEQYes VH amino acid sequence (single-letter codes, no gaps).
LSEQYes VL amino acid sequence (single-letter codes, no gaps).
CDR3Auto HCDR3 amino acid sequence. If absent, auto-extracted from HSEQ via IMGT numbering.
any label columnTraining / eval only Pass any column name to --target. See label conventions below.

Label column conventions

The label column is specified with --target <column_name>. Any column name is accepted — it is not limited to psr_filter.

Task typeLabel valuesExample column namesNotes
Binary classification 1 (PASS) / 0 (FAIL) psr_filter, sec_filter, hic_filter, ac_sins_filter, my_assay_label Any binary 0/1 column. Model outputs P(PASS) and a binary call.
Regression Continuous float psr_norm_smp, psr_norm_dna, sec_score, tm_value Use --model xgboost or transformer_lm. Model outputs a continuous predicted value; evaluated with Spearman ρ.
Tip — multi-assay Excel file You can have multiple label columns in the same file (e.g. psr_filter, sec_filter, hic_filter). Run each as a separate --target invocation and the scored output files can be merged on BARCODE.
Model YAML configuration Every model architecture (Transformer, CNN, XGBoost, RF) has a YAML config file that controls all hyperparameters. Default configs live in configs/models/. See the 📄 Model YAML reference at the end of this tutorial for full details.
1

Predict with IPI / DS1 pre-trained models

New antibodies → instant PSR/SEC risk scores — zero training required
When to use You have new antibody sequences and want PSR or SEC risk scores immediately using the IPI or DS1 pre-trained models (all architectures × all PLMs included).
i
Single PLM
Single PLM prediction
python predict_developability.py \
    --target  psr_filter \
    --lm      ablang \
    --model   transformer_lm \
    --db      data/ipi_psr_trainset.xlsx \
    --predict my_antibodies.xlsx
ii
All PLMs
All PLMs at once (recommended for publication)
python predict_developability.py \
    --target psr_filter \
    --lm     ablang,antiberty,antiberta2,antiberta2-cssp,igbert \
    --model  transformer_lm \
    --db     data/ipi_psr_trainset.xlsx \
    --predict my_antibodies.xlsx
iii
PSR+SEC
PSR + SEC simultaneously
# PSR
python predict_developability.py --target psr_filter --lm ablang \
    --model transformer_lm --db data/ipi_psr_trainset.xlsx --predict cohort.xlsx

# SEC  (same cohort, different pre-trained model)
python predict_developability.py --target sec_filter --lm ablang \
    --model transformer_lm --db data/ipi_sec_trainset.xlsx --predict cohort.xlsx
📂 Output files & how to interpret them

Output file index

File / LocationContentsWhen created
{input}_pred_{target}_{lm}_{model}_{db}.xlsx Original input + added score columns (see below) Every predict run
build/pretrained_models/*_score_distribution.tiff P(PASS) histogram — PASS vs FAIL separation If --test_target is provided
build/pretrained_models/*_roc.tiff ROC curve with AUC, train + validation curves If --test_target is provided
build/pretrained_models/*_biophysical_distribution.tiff CDR3 length / charge / hydrophobicity distributions Every predict run
build/pretrained_models/*_shap_bar.tiff
build/pretrained_models/*_shap_beeswarm.tiff		 Global SHAP feature importance (bar) and direction (beeswarm) XGBoost / RF models only
build/pretrained_models/*_shap_waterfall_Ab{N}.tiff Per-antibody SHAP waterfall — why a single antibody was scored XGBoost / RF + --test_target
build/pretrained_models/*_ig_heatmap.tiff Integrated Gradients CDR3 attribution heatmap Transformer / CNN models only

Scored prediction file — added columns

Your original input file is returned with the following columns appended for each PLM run (illustrated here for transformer_lm + ablang + IPI PSR trainset):

Column nameTypeInterpretation
transformer_lm_ablang_ipi_psr_trainset_score float 0–1 P(PASS) probability. The main output. Values closer to 1 = higher confidence PASS. Use this continuous score for ranking or correlation analyses.
transformer_lm_ablang_ipi_psr_trainset_label 0 / 1 Binary call at fixed threshold 0.5. 1 = predicted PASS, 0 = predicted FAIL. Conservative; use when a simple yes/no classification is needed.
transformer_lm_ablang_ipi_psr_trainset_optimallabel 0 / 1 Binary call at the optimal threshold (maximises F1 on the training set). Preferred over fixed-0.5 threshold for PSR/SEC decisions.
Tip — multi-PLM consensus When all five PLMs are run, calculate the mean score across PLMs per antibody. A mean ≥ 0.5 with ≥ 4/5 PLMs calling PASS is a high-confidence PASS call. Discard antibodies where PLMs disagree strongly (max – min score > 0.35).

Score distribution plot

Auto-generated when --test_target is provided. Shows P(PASS) score density for the PASS and FAIL cohorts separately.

Fig A. Score distribution — three PLMs on the same cohort. Each panel is one PLM (AbLang2, AntiBERTa2, IgBert). Blue = PASS cohort; orange = FAIL cohort. Dashed line = decision threshold (0.27 for PSR).

What to look for: (1) Separation — the two distributions should be well-separated. Overlapping distributions indicate the model struggles on this cohort. (2) Threshold placement — the dashed line should sit between the two peaks. If most PASS antibodies fall left of the threshold, consider lowering it. (3) Consistency across PLMs — if one PLM gives a very different distribution, investigate whether that PLM embedding is appropriate for your sequences.

Biophysical property distributions

Auto-generated for every predict run. Shows CDR3 length, net charge and hydrophobicity distributions split by true label (if --test_target provided) or by predicted label.

Fig B. Biophysical property distributions — PASS vs FAIL cohort. Blue = PASS; orange = FAIL.

CDR3 Length: Longer CDR3s correlate with higher polyreactivity risk. If your FAIL cohort has significantly longer CDR3s, CDR3 shortening is a viable engineering strategy.
CDR3 Charge: Positive (basic) CDR3s drive non-specific binding. A left-shifted (more negative) charge in the PASS cohort is expected for PSR. Flag antibodies with very high positive charge.
Hydrophobicity: Elevated CDR3 hydrophobicity strongly predicts PSR failure. Substituting hydrophobic residues with charged or polar ones is a proven rescue strategy.

SHAP waterfall — per-antibody explanation (XGBoost / RF only)

Generated when --test_target is provided. One waterfall per antibody, ranked by prediction error. Shows exactly which features pushed a single antibody's score above or below the base value E[f(x)].

Fig C. SHAP waterfall — single antibody example (XGBoost · AbLang2).

How to read: The grey bar at the bottom is the base value E[f(x)] — the average P(PASS) across the training set. Each coloured bar shows one feature's contribution: red bars push the score upward (toward PASS); blue bars push it downward (toward FAIL). The dashed vertical line is the final predicted score f(x).

In this example: CDR3 charge and k-mer GGY are the dominant FAIL drivers (blue bars on the left), while emb_521 (VH-CDR3) and emb_774 partially compensate (red bars). The final score 0.57 crosses the PASS threshold. This antibody is at the boundary — investigate its CDR3 charge and GGY motif before advancing.

Multi-PLM consensus chart

When running all five PLMs (--lm all), generate a consensus chart by plotting all per-PLM scores side-by-side. Antibodies where all PLMs agree are high-confidence hits.

Fig D. Multi-PLM consensus scores — 18 candidate antibodies. Each colour = one PLM. Dashed line = 0.5 decision threshold.

Interpretation guide: (1) High consensus PASS (all bars above 0.5) — strong candidates; advance to wet-lab. (2) High consensus FAIL (all bars below 0.5) — deprioritise unless structural rationale exists. (3) Split consensus (mixed above/below) — inspect SHAP waterfall and biophysical properties; consider running mutagenesis (App 4) to identify possible rescue mutations. (4) One PLM outlier — check if that PLM embedding was trained on similar sequence space; consider excluding it from the consensus call.

Integrated Gradients heatmap (Transformer / CNN only)

Generated automatically when the model is transformer_lm or cnn. Unlike SHAP (which attributes to abstract embedding dimensions), IG directly attributes to specific amino acids at specific CDR3/VH positions.

Fig E. Integrated Gradients heatmap — CDR3 attribution. Rows = 20 amino acids. Columns = CDR3 positions H1–H14. Box outline = wild-type residue. Red = this residue at this position increases P(PASS). Blue = decreases P(PASS).

Interpreting the heatmap for App 1 (pre-trained predict): (1) Positions with a blue wild-type cell are liability hotspots — the natural residue drives polyreactivity. Flag these for mutagenesis (App 4). (2) Positions with a red wild-type cell are anchors — preserve these residues in any engineering campaign. (3) White / near-zero cells are neutral — mutations there carry low risk.
Summary — output interpretation workflow
  1. Scored file — rank by _score; flag antibodies near the threshold (0.4–0.6) for follow-up
  2. Score distribution — validate that PASS/FAIL populations are well-separated; if not, the pre-trained model may be out-of-domain
  3. Biophysical distributions — identify bulk biophysical trends driving failure (long CDR3, high charge, high hydrophobicity)
  4. SHAP waterfall — for individual borderline antibodies, identify the top 2–3 feature drivers
  5. IG heatmap — pinpoint the exact CDR3 positions responsible for liability
  6. Multi-PLM consensus chart — use as final gating criterion before expensive wet-lab experiments
2

Build new ML models from your own dataset

Full pipeline: embedding → CDR3-cluster split → k-fold → train → predict → interpret
Fig 1. Six-step pipeline for building a new model. Steps 1–2 are run once per dataset; steps 3–6 iterate per architecture and PLM.
1
Embed
Generate PLM embeddings

Embeddings are cached as {file}.{lm}.emb.csv — subsequent runs reuse the cache automatically.

# One PLM
python predict_developability.py \
    --build-embedding my_trainset.xlsx --lm ablang

# All PLMs at once
python predict_developability.py \
    --build-embedding my_trainset.xlsx --lm all
2
Split
CDR3-cluster stratified split

Groups antibodies by HCDR3 sequence similarity (Levenshtein ≥ 0.8 identity) — prevents data leakage across train / val.

python predict_developability.py \
    --target psr_filter \
    --db     my_trainset.xlsx \
    --split  0.8
# → my_trainset_train.xlsx (80%)
# → my_trainset_val.xlsx   (20%)
Why CDR3 clustering matters Standard random split leaks similar antibodies across train/val — inflating AUC artificially. CDR3-cluster split gives a true out-of-distribution estimate matching real discovery scenarios.
3
K-fold
K-fold cross-validation
# Transformer + AbLang2
python predict_developability.py \
    --kfold 10 --target psr_filter \
    --lm ablang --model transformer_lm \
    --db my_trainset.xlsx

# XGBoost + IgBert
python predict_developability.py \
    --kfold 10 --target psr_filter \
    --lm igbert --model xgboost \
    --db my_trainset.xlsx

Sample output: Mean AUC = 0.961 ± 0.006   Acc=0.897   F1=0.906

Customise model hyperparameters Before training, optionally create a custom YAML config to override defaults — e.g. increase num_layers for a large dataset, or tune max_depth for XGBoost. See the 📄 Model YAML reference for all options. Pass it via: --config configs/models/my_config.yaml
4
Train
Train final model on full dataset
python predict_developability.py \
    --train  --target psr_filter \
    --lm ablang --model transformer_lm \
    --db my_trainset.xlsx
# → FINAL_psr_filter_ablang_transformer_lm_my_trainset.pt
5
Predict
Predict on new antibodies
python predict_developability.py \
    --target psr_filter --lm ablang --model transformer_lm \
    --db my_trainset.xlsx --predict new_cohort.xlsx
Fig 2. Score distribution (auto-generated). Well-separated PASS/FAIL distributions indicate good model performance. Wide overlap suggests more training data is needed.
6
Interpret
SHAP / IG interpretation

SHAP (XGBoost / RF) and Integrated Gradients (Transformer / CNN) run automatically. See Section ⑤ for a detailed guide.

# Predict validation set + evaluate SHAP
python predict_developability.py \
    --target psr_filter --lm ablang --model xgboost \
    --db my_trainset_train.xlsx \
    --predict my_trainset_val.xlsx \
    --test_target psr_filter
3

Dataset quality, motif discovery and assay correlation analysis

Understand what drives developability and how well ML scores correlate with raw assay measurements
When to use Validate that your dataset is informative, discover sequence motifs and biophysical drivers, and quantify how well ML scores correlate with continuous assay values across multiple assays and models.

Commands

# 1. Split dataset
python predict_developability.py --target psr_filter \
    --db my_dataset.xlsx --split 0.8

# 2. Train k-mer model (CDR3 amino acid frequency — fully interpretable)
python predict_developability.py --kfold 10 --target psr_filter \
    --lm kmer --model xgboost --db my_dataset.xlsx

# 3. Train biophysical model (charge, hydrophobicity, CDR3 length — no PLM)
python predict_developability.py --kfold 10 --target psr_filter \
    --lm biophysical --model rf --db my_dataset.xlsx

# 4. Full correlation + t-SNE + boxplot analysis
#    --assay  : one or more continuous assay columns in the file
#    --target : binary label column (for boxplot stratification)
#    --tsne-source : embeddings | assay | both
python utils/developability_correlation.py \
    --files  my_dataset_val_pred_psr_filter_ablang_xgboost_my_dataset_train.xlsx \
    --assay  psr_norm_smp psr_norm_dna psr_norm_avidin \
    --target psr_filter \
    --tsne-source both \
    --out    results/dataset_quality

Output files

FileContents
*_results.csv Spearman ρ and Pearson r for every model × assay combination — sortable table
*_scatter_{assay}.tiff Scatter plot: ML P(PASS) score vs continuous assay value, with regression line
*_heatmap_spearman.tiff Model × assay Spearman ρ heatmap — compare all models at a glance
*_boxplot_{target}.tiff Raw assay distributions stratified by ML predicted label (PASS vs FAIL)
*_tsne_{target}.tiff t-SNE of PLM embeddings coloured by true assay label
*_tsne_labels.tiff Same t-SNE coloured by ML predicted score (continuous gradient)
📊 How to interpret assay correlation outputs

ML score vs continuous assay — scatter plots

Each scatter plot shows one model's P(PASS) score on the x-axis against a continuous assay measurement on the y-axis. Spearman ρ and Pearson r are annotated on each panel.

Fig G. ML P(PASS) score vs continuous assay values (PSR SMP, PSR DNA, PSR Avidin). Blue = true PASS (assay < threshold); orange = true FAIL (assay ≥ threshold). Dashed regression line. Red dotted line = assay decision threshold. Grey dotted line = ML threshold (0.5).

Interpreting Spearman ρ:
ρ rangeInterpretationAction
> 0.70Strong correlation — model captures continuous assay wellProceed to training / deployment
0.50 – 0.70Moderate — model captures rank order but misses subtle variationConsider regression mode (task: regression in YAML)
0.30 – 0.50Weak — some signal, possibly limited by dataset size or assay noiseExpand dataset; check assay reproducibility
< 0.30No meaningful correlation — model may be out-of-domainRe-train with in-domain data; check sequence quality

Pearson r vs Spearman ρ: Pearson r assumes linearity — use it when the scatter appears linear. Spearman ρ is rank-based and more robust to outliers and non-linear monotonic relationships. For PSR/SEC data, Spearman ρ is the primary metric.

Multi-model × multi-assay Spearman ρ heatmap

Shows Spearman ρ for every model–PLM combination (rows) against every assay (columns). Box outlines mark the best-performing model for each assay.

Fig H. Spearman ρ heatmap — all model × assay combinations. Green = high correlation; red = low. Box outline = best model for that assay column.

Reading the heatmap: (1) Row comparison — which model architecture + PLM gives the highest ρ overall? (2) Column comparison — which assay is best predicted by ML? Assays with low ρ across all models may have high biological noise or poor assay reproducibility. (3) Diagonal pattern — if PSR SMP and PSR DNA columns are highly correlated, the assays are measuring the same biology, and a single model can serve both. (4) Off-diagonal drop — if ρ is high for PSR but low for AC-SINS, a separate model trained on AC-SINS data is needed.

Assay value distributions by ML predicted label

Boxplots of raw assay values split by ML predicted label (PASS / FAIL). T-test stars indicate whether the separation is statistically significant.

Fig I. Assay value distributions stratified by ML predicted label. Blue = ML predicts PASS; orange = ML predicts FAIL. Red dashed line = assay threshold. Stars = T-test significance (*** p < 0.001).

What to look for: (1) Good separation — ML PASS group has significantly lower assay values than ML FAIL group. This confirms the model has learned the correct biology. (2) Overlap at the boundary — some overlap near the threshold is expected and normal. Large overlap (non-significant T-test) suggests the model is not discriminating well. (3) Outliers in ML PASS group — antibodies with a high ML PASS score but a high assay value are false positives. Investigate these for unusual sequence features. (4) Outliers in ML FAIL group — antibodies with low assay values but ML predicts FAIL are false negatives. Check if they have unusual CDR3 sequences that fall outside the training distribution.

t-SNE embedding visualisation

t-SNE projects the high-dimensional PLM embeddings onto 2D, revealing cluster structure. Two views are generated: coloured by true label, and by continuous ML score.

Fig J. t-SNE of PLM embeddings — true label (left) and ML P(PASS) score (right).

Left panel (true label): If PASS (blue) and FAIL (orange) form distinct clusters, the PLM embedding space naturally separates the two classes — a good sign for model generalisation. Mixed clusters indicate the assay phenotype is driven by subtle sequence features not well-captured by the embedding.

Right panel (ML score gradient): The colour gradient should match the cluster structure seen in the left panel. If green (high P(PASS)) and red (low P(PASS)) regions do not align with the true-label clusters, the model may be overfitting to spurious features.

Practical use: Identify regions of the t-SNE where the model makes systematic errors (e.g. a blue true-PASS cluster coloured red by the model). Antibodies in those regions are candidates for targeted re-training or additional data collection.
Correlation analysis decision workflow
  1. Run developability_correlation.py on your scored validation set
  2. Check Spearman ρ — if ρ > 0.65 across all assays, proceed confidently
  3. Use the heatmap to select the best model architecture per assay
  4. Use the boxplot to confirm statistical separation of PASS / FAIL groups
  5. Use t-SNE to identify problematic clusters and guide data collection
  6. If ρ < 0.50 for an assay, consider switching to regression mode (task: regression)
4

In silico Mutagenesis Modelling (Prescreening)

Scan every CDR3 substitution to guide wet-lab engineering before synthesis
When to use You have a lead antibody with PSR or SEC liability and want to identify which CDR3 point mutations are predicted to improve developability — before synthesising hundreds of variants.

Single target

python predict_developability.py \
    --target psr_filter --lm ablang --model transformer_lm \
    --db data/ipi_psr_trainset.xlsx \
    --predict candidates.xlsx --mutagenesis 50

Multi-target: PSR + SEC simultaneously

python predict_developability.py --target psr_filter --lm ablang \
    --model transformer_lm --db data/ipi_psr_trainset.xlsx \
    --predict candidates.xlsx --mutagenesis 50

python predict_developability.py --target sec_filter --lm ablang \
    --model transformer_lm --db data/ipi_sec_trainset.xlsx \
    --predict candidates.xlsx --mutagenesis 50

Multi-PLM consensus (recommended for lead decisions)

for LM in ablang antiberta2 igbert; do
    python predict_developability.py \
        --target psr_filter --lm $LM --model transformer_lm \
        --db data/ipi_psr_trainset.xlsx \
        --predict candidates.xlsx --mutagenesis 50
done
📊 How to interpret the mutagenesis heatmap

Heatmap anatomy

Fig 3. In silico mutagenesis heatmap — annotated example. Rows = 20 standard amino acids. Columns = CDR3 positions H1–H14. Yellow-boxed cells = wild-type (WT) residue at each position. Cell value = predicted P(PASS) score for that amino acid at that position. Colour = Δ P(PASS) relative to WT (red = improvement, blue = worsening).

Title fields explained

Title fieldShown whenMeaning
Antibody ID (e.g. Ab_0042) Always — taken from the BARCODE column Identifies which antibody the heatmap belongs to
Actual label (e.g. FAIL (psr_filter = 0)) When the prediction file contains the --target column Ground-truth outcome from the wet-lab assay — lets you immediately see whether the model was correct
WT P(PASS) score (e.g. 0.31) Always The model's predicted P(PASS) for the unmodified WT sequence. The starting point for all Δ calculations in the heatmap

Reading the cell values

Every cell shows the absolute predicted P(PASS) score for that amino acid substitution. The cell colour encodes Δ P(PASS) = (mutant score) − (WT score):

Red cell (Δ > 0)

  • Substituting this amino acid at this position increases P(PASS)
  • The brighter the red, the larger the improvement
  • If the cell score ≥ threshold and the WT score is below, this mutation may rescue the antibody
  • High-priority engineering target when WT cell is blue and this cell is deeply red

Blue cell (Δ < 0)

  • Substitution decreases P(PASS) — avoid this change
  • If the WT cell is blue = wild-type residue is a liability driver at this position
  • A blue WT cell next to red cells = clear engineering opportunity
  • Deep blue = strong liability — prioritise for mutagenesis

Yellow-boxed (WT) cell

The yellow-highlighted cell in each column marks the wild-type residue. Its score equals the WT P(PASS) score in the title. Use it as the reference point:

WT cell colourScore vs thresholdInterpretation
🟡 Yellow / neutralScore near WT (Δ ≈ 0) No strong signal at this position — low engineering priority
Warm red-yellowWT score high WT residue is already favourable — preserve it during engineering
Cool blue-yellowWT score low (< threshold) Liability hotspot — the natural residue drives FAIL; look for red cells in this column
Prescreening workflow 1. Run mutagenesis on lead CDR3  →  2. Identify red hotspot positions  →  3. Cross-check PSR + SEC heatmaps  →  4. Shortlist 5–10 variants with PLM consensus  →  5. Synthesise and measure — wet-lab confirmation
⑤

How to read SHAP and Integrated Gradients figures

Interpretation guide for all auto-generated explainability plots

SHAP — SHapley Additive exPlanations

Generated automatically for XGBoost and Random Forest models. Three plot types are produced.

Bar plot — global importance

  • Shows mean |SHAP value| per feature across all antibodies
  • Top features have largest average impact on prediction
  • Red bars = high impact; grey bars = low contribution
  • Use to assess PLM vs biophysical reliance

Beeswarm — direction of effect

  • Each dot = one antibody
  • X-axis: SHAP value (left = increases FAIL; right = increases PASS)
  • Dot colour: feature value (red = high, blue = low)
  • Blue dot on right → low feature value increases PASS
Fig 4. SHAP bar chart. PLM embedding dimensions dominate, followed by CDR3 charge and length. Red dashed line marks the high-impact threshold (0.05).
Fig 5. SHAP beeswarm. For CDR3 charge (row 4), blue dots (low charge) cluster to the left — meaning low-charge CDR3s tend to FAIL. Use this plot to validate model biology before trusting predictions.

Integrated Gradients — Transformer and CNN models

IG attributes directly to specific amino acids at specific CDR3/VH positions — biologically interpretable at the residue level.

Red cells = beneficial

  • Residue at this position increases P(PASS)
  • Wild-type is red → natural residue is developability-favourable
  • Non-WT red cell → consider engineering that substitution
  • Consistent red across PLMs = high-confidence call

Blue cells = deleterious

  • Residue at this position increases FAIL risk
  • Wild-type is blue → natural residue drives polyreactivity — engineering target
  • Positions with many blue cells are liability hotspots
  • Cross-validate with mutagenesis heatmap
Fig 6. Integrated Gradients heatmap. Rows = 20 amino acids. Columns = CDR3 positions H1–H14. Box = wild-type residue. Example: position H4 — wild-type R (boxed, row 14) scores highly positive. Blue cells at H4 for I and A indicate deleterious substitutions. Position H11 is neutral — model insensitive to mutations there.

Combining SHAP + IG + Mutagenesis

PlotModelWhat it tells youBest used for
SHAP barXGBoost · RFWhich feature types matter globallyAssessing PLM vs biophysical reliance
SHAP beeswarmXGBoost · RFDirection of feature effectsValidating model biology
SHAP heatmapXGBoost · RFPer-antibody SHAP profileFinding outliers; clustering
IG heatmapTransformer · CNNPer-position amino acid attributionIdentifying liability residues
MutagenesisAnyCounterfactual: ΔP(PASS) per substitutionEngineering candidate identification
Recommended workflow 1. SHAP bar — confirm model uses biologically sensible features  →  2. SHAP beeswarm — validate directions  →  3. IG heatmap on flagged antibodies — pinpoint liability residues  →  4. Mutagenesis on flagged CDR3 positions — find engineering solutions  →  5. Cross-validate across multiple PLMs — retain only consensus predictions
📄

Model YAML configuration reference

Hyperparameter reference for all four model architectures

Each model architecture has a YAML configuration file that controls all hyperparameters. Default configs live in configs/models/. Override any parameter by passing --config your_config.yaml to the CLI.
Config search order (later overrides earlier): built-in defaults → --config file → explicit CLI flags.

Model YAML configuration

Each model architecture has a YAML configuration file that controls all hyperparameters. The default configs live in configs/models/. Override any parameter by passing --config your_config.yaml to the CLI. Below are the default configs and explanations for each model.

TF
transformer_lm
Transformer (PLM embeddings) — configs/models/transformer_lm.yaml
model:
  type: transformer_lm

  # ── Transformer encoder ──────────────────────────────────
  hidden_dim: 256          # Embedding projection size before the transformer
  num_heads: 8             # Multi-head self-attention heads (hidden_dim must be divisible)
  num_layers: 4            # Number of encoder layers
  dropout: 0.1             # Dropout probability (encoder + classifier head)
  ffn_dim: 512             # Feed-forward network intermediate size

  # ── Input ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  max_seq_len: 512         # Max token length (sequences are padded/truncated)
  pooling: cls             # cls | mean | max — how to aggregate over sequence positions

  # ── Training ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  epochs: 80               # Max training epochs (early stopping applies)
  patience: 12             # Early stopping patience (epochs without val improvement)
  batch_size: 32
  learning_rate: 1.0e-4
  weight_decay: 1.0e-5
  optimizer: adamw         # adamw | adam | sgd
  scheduler: cosine        # cosine | step | none
  warmup_steps: 500

  # ── Classification head ──────────────────────────────────
  task: classification     # classification | regression
  num_classes: 2           # 2 for binary; 1 for regression
  class_weights: auto      # auto | none | [w0, w1]  (auto balances class imbalance)
Key parameters to tune num_layers and hidden_dim — increase for larger datasets (>2000 sequences).   class_weights: auto — crucial for imbalanced PSR datasets (typically 70/30 PASS/FAIL). pooling: mean — often better than cls for short CDR3-only inputs.
CN
cnn
CNN (PLM embeddings) — configs/models/cnn.yaml
model:
  type: cnn

  # ── Convolutional layers ─────────────────────────────────
  filters: [128, 256, 256]    # Number of filters per conv layer
  kernel_sizes: [3, 5, 7]     # Kernel widths (captures motifs of different lengths)
  pool: max                   # max | avg — global pooling after each conv block
  activation: relu            # relu | gelu | swish

  # ── Classifier head ─────────────────────────────────────
  fc_dims: [256, 128]         # Fully-connected layers after the conv stack
  dropout: 0.2

  # ── Training ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  task: classification        # classification | regression
  epochs: 100
  patience: 15
  batch_size: 64
  learning_rate: 5.0e-4
  optimizer: adam
  scheduler: step
  step_size: 30               # Reduce LR every N epochs (step scheduler only)
  gamma: 0.5                  # LR reduction factor (step scheduler only)
  class_weights: auto
Key parameters to tune kernel_sizes — add larger kernels (e.g. 9, 11) to capture longer motifs. filters — for small datasets (<500 sequences) reduce to [64, 128] to avoid overfitting.
XGB
xgboost
XGBoost — configs/models/xgboost.yaml
model:
  type: xgboost

  # ── Tree structure ────────────────────────────────────────
  n_estimators: 500           # Number of boosting rounds
  max_depth: 6                # Maximum tree depth (lower = less overfitting)
  min_child_weight: 5         # Minimum sum of instance weight in a leaf
  subsample: 0.8              # Row subsampling per tree
  colsample_bytree: 0.8       # Feature subsampling per tree
  colsample_bylevel: 1.0      # Feature subsampling per split level

  # ── Regularisation ───────────────────────────────────────
  learning_rate: 0.05         # eta — step size shrinkage
  gamma: 0.1                  # Minimum loss reduction to split a node
  reg_alpha: 0.0              # L1 regularisation on leaf weights
  reg_lambda: 1.0             # L2 regularisation on leaf weights

  # ── Training ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  task: classification        # classification | regression
  eval_metric: auc            # auc | logloss | rmse | mae
  early_stopping_rounds: 30
  scale_pos_weight: auto      # auto | 1.0  (auto = neg_count / pos_count)

  # ── SHAP ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  shap: true                  # Generate SHAP plots automatically
  shap_max_display: 20        # Top-N features shown in bar / beeswarm plots
  shap_waterfall_n: 10        # Number of per-antibody waterfall plots to save
Key parameters to tune n_estimators + early_stopping_rounds — set n_estimators high (1000) and rely on early stopping. max_depth: 4 — reduce if overfitting on small datasets. shap: true — required to get SHAP waterfall and beeswarm output files.
RF
rf
Random Forest — configs/models/rf.yaml
model:
  type: rf

  # ── Forest structure ─────────────────────────────────────
  n_estimators: 500           # Number of trees
  max_depth: null             # null = grow fully; set integer to limit depth
  min_samples_split: 5        # Minimum samples to split a node
  min_samples_leaf: 2         # Minimum samples in a leaf
  max_features: sqrt          # sqrt | log2 | float (fraction) | auto
  bootstrap: true             # Sample with replacement

  # ── Class handling ───────────────────────────────────────
  task: classification        # classification | regression
  class_weight: balanced      # balanced | balanced_subsample | null
                              # balanced = weight inversely proportional to class frequency

  # ── SHAP ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
  shap: true
  shap_max_display: 20
  shap_waterfall_n: 10

  # ── Reproducibility ──────────────────────────────────────
  random_state: 42
Key parameters to tune max_features: 0.3 — often better than sqrt for high-dimensional PLM embeddings. class_weight: balanced_subsample — rebalances each bootstrap sample independently; more aggressive than balanced for strongly imbalanced data.

Using a custom YAML config

# Pass a custom config to any workflow
python predict_developability.py \
    --target my_assay_label \
    --lm ablang --model xgboost \
    --db my_trainset.xlsx \
    --config configs/models/xgboost_tuned.yaml \
    --kfold 10

# Config search order (later overrides earlier):
# 1. Built-in defaults (configs/models/{model}.yaml)
# 2. --config file
# 3. CLI flags (--epochs, --lr, etc.) if supported
Quick-start recipe — new assay with binary label
  1. Add your label column to the Excel file (e.g. hic_filter with 0/1 values)
  2. Run --build-embedding to pre-cache PLM embeddings
  3. Run --kfold 10 --target hic_filter --lm ablang --model xgboost
  4. Inspect SHAP bar to confirm model uses biologically relevant features
  5. If AUC > 0.85, train final model with --train
  6. Predict new candidates with --predict new_cohort.xlsx
📖

Common flags reference

FlagDescriptionExample
--targetLabel columnpsr_filter, sec_filter, psr_norm_smp
--lmPLM (comma-separated)ablang,igbert, all, biophysical, kmer
--modelML architecturetransformer_lm, xgboost, rf, cnn
--dbTraining databasedata/ipi_psr_trainset.xlsx
--predictFile to scorenew_cohort.xlsx
--kfold NN-fold HCDR3-stratified CV--kfold 10
--trainTrain final model on full --db
--split FCDR3-cluster stratified split--split 0.8
--val FILEHeld-out validation during training
--test_targetTrue label in prediction filepsr_filter
--mutagenesis NCDR3 mutagenesis, top N mutations--mutagenesis 50
--threshold TOverride score threshold--threshold 0.27
--build-embeddingPre-generate PLM embeddings
